use a group of 10 parallel agents in a loop to do all the deep research (and research of previous hackathon github repos for this particular hackathon). save this documentation. to help with grounding. This is all to help us come up with the actual idea of what to build for the hackathon and also what one single prompt we should write so claude can do it and self correct and keep iterating and improving and testing. only stop the loop once we have the solution and the solution is the idea for what to build and the prompt or prompt(s) that make claude create sub agents and goals and instructions for the full agentic loop. We're not building anything. we're creating the perfect prompt to get it done. do you follow? don't start yet

CV Logo
New Event Blast

There's a new blast for Claude Build Day:

Hi builders,

We're looking forward to seeing you at tomorrow's Build Day event at Shack15. A few updates:

We’ll be building on Opus 4.8 instead of Fable 5 due to a recent update (https://www.anthropic.com/news/fable-mythos-access).

The fireside chat with Jared Kaplan and the customer panel will no longer take place.

The updated evening program leaves more time for building and will include finalist demos, judging, and a closing celebration.

Please view all event details, including entrance instructions and schedule at: https://cerebralvalley.ai/e/claude-startups-build-day/details

Thank you for the incredible response to this event, we can’t wait to build with you tomorrow!

# **Claude Build Day Participant Guide 👩‍💻👨‍💻**

Welcome to the Claude Build Day! 👋 We’re thrilled to have you on board.

This guide is your all-in-one resource for the event, including schedule, rules, technical resources, problem statements, judging information, and more. Please read this carefully; most answers can be found here.

## **1️⃣ Your Goal – Hackathon Problem Statement**

Build something new, such as a complete, working app from a standing start. Or bring your product and point Claude at the biggest problem it's facing. Feel free to leverage [dynamic workflows](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/workflows) or other orchestration features, and let Claude carry the work. You'll get credits to cover tokens for the entire Build Day.

1. **Start with a clear brief.** What's the problem, who is it for, and what does done look like? 

2. **Kick it off.** Spend time briefing Claude: point it at your repo, answer its questions, set up your goal and rubric. Then let it go.

3. **Ship.** Deploy to a live URL and submit it with your brief, your rubric, and the session log.

**Pick a problem worth solving**

A few thought starters:

- Ship your product somewhere you've never shipped: mobile, desktop, CLI.

- The tool San Francisco or a nonprofit you love deserves, built on the public data they already publish.

- A claims, loan-servicing, or back-office workflow that takes weeks today.

- The redesign your product has needed for a year.

- The swarm: point a team of agents at your real backlog, ship the release, and demo what's new.

**Scoring Criteria:**

- Impact (35%): What's the real-world potential here? Who benefits, and how much does it matter? Could this actually become something people use? Does it fit into one of the problem statements listed above?
- Demo (35%): Is it a working and impressive demo? Does it hold up live? Does the demo prove the impact?
- Opus 4.8 Use (15%): How creatively did this team use Opus 4.8? Did they go beyond a basic integration? Did they surface capabilities that surprised even us?
- Orchestration (15%): Judged from the submitted brief, rubric, and any workflow scripts (not from which features were used). Is this orchestration simple and repeatable? Is "done" verifiable by the model without a human: a test suite, a responding URL, a rubric file it can grade against? Could another team rerun the setup tomorrow on a new problem? 

**Resources:**

- **Primary Resources**

  - [A harness for every task: dynamic workflows in Claude Code](https://claude.com/blog/a-harness-for-every-task-dynamic-workflows-in-claude-code)

  - [Claude Code Quickstart](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart)

  - [Claude API Quickstart](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/get-started)

- **Documentation**

  - [Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs)

  - [Claude API Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/home)

  - [MCP Docs](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/getting-started/intro)

  - [Agent Skills Docs](https://agentskills.io/home)

  - [Managed Agents Docs](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/managed-agents/overview)

- **Other Resources**

  - [Claude Quickstarts](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-quickstarts) - A collection of projects designed to help developers quickly get started with building deployable applications using the Claude API

  - [Claude Cookbooks](https://platform.claude.com/cookbook/) and [Cookbooks Github Repo](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cookbooks)

  - [Claude Code Best Practices](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices)

  - [Agent Skills Github Repo](https://github.com/anthropics/skills)

## **2️⃣ Getting Ready – Location & Arrival**

**📍Location:** Shack15 (Ferry Building, 1, Suite 201, San Francisco, CA 94111)

[googleMap]

**Arrival Instructions**

Due to high demand and limited capacity, please arrive at 9:00 AM when doors open. Bring your ID for check-in on the second floor. You will receive a name tag and a wristband, which must be worn at all times in the venue.

**Wi-Fi Access**

**SSID:** Claude Build Day

**Password:** problemsolvers

**Getting Here**

**Public Transit (recommended):** Take BART or Muni to the Embarcadero station — it's a 2-minute walk from there. The Ferry Building is also served by the F-Market streetcar and several Muni bus lines. SF-bound ferries from Oakland, Alameda, and Sausalito arrive right at the Ferry Building.

**Rideshare & Waymo:** Set your destination to "Ferry Building." Drop-off and pick-up are on The Embarcadero, directly in front of the building.

**Parking:** Limited paid parking is available in the Ferry Building garage (entrance on Washington St), with the Pier 1 lot as the nearest alternative. You can also check [SpotHero](https://spothero.com/) for availability at nearby lots.

*Parking is limited — we strongly recommend taking public transit or rideshare.*

## **3️⃣ Connect with the Community - Discord**

Join us on Discord to meet other participants, get official updates, begin forming teams:

Getting Started:

- Join the Claude Discord server [here](https://anthropic.com/discord)

  [linkEmbed]

- **Please note:** We will assign a custom role to your account so you will be able to see the hackathon specific channels.

- **Introduce yourself:** In #intros, share who you are, the skills you bring, and what project you’re looking to build.

- **Create a Team:** In #team-search, find teammates before the hackathon (maximum team size of four)

**Key Channels:**

- #general: Socialize and meet other hackers.

- #rules: On the day rules spanning from registration, product building, and pitching.

- #announcements: Official updates and reminders from the CV Team.

- #intros: Introduce yourself and what you’re doing to everyone!

- #team-search: Find teammates before the hackathon (maximum team size of **four**).

- #questions: Ask any general questions to the CV and Anthropic team..

- #social: Share your hackathon posts for other participants to reshare/interact!

- #credit-questions: Request support from the CV team on obtaining your $500 in Claude API credits.

## **4️⃣ Share Your Builds - Media Guide**

- Pick a post below (we've got X options + a LinkedIn one) — feel free to make it your own. 

- Tag @claudeai, @claudedevs, and @cerebral_valley

- Attach the "Claude Build Day" graphic to your post — it works on both X and LinkedIn.

\
**Posting to Twitter/X**

Building with Claude Code today as part of @claudeai, @claudedevs, and @cerebral_valley's Claude Build Day. 

*<Describe what you’re building here>*

**Posting to LinkedIn**

I'm participating in the Live Claude Build Day with Anthropic and Cerebral Valley. It's a one-day challenge to build something with Claude Code and I’m building <describe here>!

## **5️⃣ Schedule Overview**

- **9:00 AM:** Doors Open & Breakfast – *please arrive promptly!*

- **10:00 AM:** Welcome & Kick-Off

- **10:30 AM:** Hackathon Begins

- **1:00 PM:** Lunch

- **5:00 PM:** Submissions Due

- **6:15 PM:** Finalists Announced – *please be respectful as teams demo!*

- **7:45 PM:** Winners Announced

- **7:45–10:00 PM:** Closing Celebration – *beverages and dinner served*

- **10:00 PM:** Doors Close

## **6️⃣ Hackathon Rules**

- **Open Source:** Project repositories **must be public**.

- **Team Size:** A **maximum of four** team members per team. Solo participants are allowed.

- **Demo Requirements:** Your demo **must only highlight the specific features, code, and functionality that your team built during the hackathon**. 

  - Judges must be able to clearly identify what was created during the event.

  - Failure to clearly identify your original contributions will result in immediate disqualification.

- **Technologies & Projects:** You’re welcome to bring in prior projects to augment with the use of Claude during the hackathon.

- **Own Work:** Failure to clearly distinguish your contributions will result in immediate disqualification.

- **Banned Projects:** Projects will be **disqualified** if they: violate legal, ethical, or platform policies, use code, data, or assets you do not have the rights to.

### Prohibited Projects — STRICTLY NO:

- AI Mental Health Advisor

- Basic RAG Applications

- Streamlit Applications

- Image Analyzers

- “AI for Education” Chatbot

- AI Job Application Screener

- AI Nutrition Coach

- Personality Analyzers

- Medical advice bots

- Any project where a dashboard is the main feature

- Sports analyzers or coaches

- Projects will be disqualified if they: violate legal, ethical, or platform policies, use code, data, or assets you do not have the rights to.

## **7️⃣ Claude-Provided Resources**

$500 in credits, with 24hr expiry. Link to credits will be provided day of event

[A harness for every task: dynamic workflows in Claude Code](https://claude.com/blog/a-harness-for-every-task-dynamic-workflows-in-claude-code)

## **8️⃣ Submission Process**

Teams should submit [**here**](https://cerebralvalley.ai/e/claude-startups-build-day/hackathon/submit) at **5:00 PM sharp**. In the submission form, you will submit a short one minute demo video. This should be a video highlighting the specific features, code, and functionality that your team built during the hackathon.

[linkEmbed]

*Please double check that your build repository is public, your demo link is accessible, and all team members have been added to the submission page.*

**A Note to Founders/Startups:**

We understand that some of you are building with existing products. You're welcome to extend what you may already have built, but the final submission to this hackathon must be an **open source public GitHub repository containing any and all code that you present in your demo** to allow our team and Anthropic's team to review.

This means that your project must be a standalone product or tangential feature that can be extracted into a separate repository and shared with us. The GitHub links will be kept internal to our judging team and never shared publicly.

## **9️⃣ Judging Process**

Judging will take place in two rounds.

**Round One:** Submissions will be reviewed asynchronously by the Anthropic team based on the materials submitted, including your demo video and GitHub repository. Finalist teams will be selected from this review.

**Round Two:** Top 6 teams will present on stage to a panel of judges & all attendees. Each team will have 3 minutes to live demo their project, followed by 1-2 minutes of Q&A. The same criteria as above will be used, though with equal weighting for each category.

Round 2 presentation format: Presentations should be 3 minutes long showcasing what you built. Please use the following slide as your only slide (introducing your team) [\[LINK\]](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1LS1yLBViZmF3on8LIPrfSiXIFKyMfwI2bUkIC5c7et0/edit?slide=id.g3eed5c6670d_0_712#slide=id.g3eed5c6670d_0_712). 

Any other slides are discouraged - we want to see what you built (code and product on screen), how you built it, and the problem you're solving. In your presentation, you should show how you directed Claude and how it verified its own work: walk us through what you gave the model (your brief, rubric, tests, or workflow scripts) and, if you can, the moment it caught and fixed a failure on its own.

## **🔟 Prizes**

**First Place:** $100,000 API credits

**Second Place:** $40,000 API credits

**Third Place:** $10,000 API credits

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- last30days:last30days: Research what people actually say about any topic in the last 30 days across Reddit, X, YouTube, TikTok, Hacker News, Polymarket, GitHub, and the web.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- last30days:last30days: Research what people actually say about any topic in the last 30 days. Pulls posts and engagement from Reddit, X, YouTube, TikTok, Hacker News, Polymarket, GitHub, and the web.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- claude-in-chrome: Automates your Chrome browser to interact with web pages - clicking elements, filling forms, capturing screenshots, reading console logs, and navigating sites. Opens pages in new tabs within your existing Chrome session. Requires site-level permissions before executing (configured in the extension). - When the user wants to interact with web pages, automate browser tasks, capture screenshots, read console logs, or perform any browser-based actions. Always invoke BEFORE attempting to use any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tools.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Design perfect hackathon prompt with parallel agent research

content.text:
Yes, I follow. Let me play it back to make sure I've got it exactly right before I touch anything.

## What I understand

**The event:** Claude Build Day (Cerebral Valley + Anthropic) at Shack15, tomorrow. One-day hackathon, building on **Opus 4.8**. Doors 9AM, hacking 10:30AM, submissions due 5PM. Public repo required, ≤4 person teams, 1-min demo video, live URL.

**Scoring that actually matters** (this is the grounding for everything):
- Impact 35% — real-world potential, fits a problem statement
- Demo 35% — working, impressive, holds up live
- Opus 4.8 Use 15% — creative, beyond basic integration, surfaces surprising capability
- **Orchestration 15%** — judged from the *brief, rubric, and workflow scripts*. Is "done" verifiable by the model without a human (test suite, responding URL, rubric file it grades against)? Could another team rerun it tomorrow on a new problem?
- Hard constraints: the long **prohibited list** (no basic RAG, no Streamlit, no dashboards-as-the-main-feature, no chatbots-for-X, etc.).

**The task you're giving me — and what it is NOT:**
- I am **NOT building the product.** Not writing app code.
- I AM running **10 parallel research agents in a loop**, iterating until convergence, doing deep research + mining **prior hackathon GitHub repos** (for this and comparable Anthropic/CV hackathons) to ground us.
- **Save all that research as documentation** (for grounding).
- The loop **only stops when we have the SOLUTION**, where "solution" = two things:
  1. **The idea** — exactly what to build for this hackathon (one that scores high on all 4 criteria and dodges the prohibited list).
  2. **The prompt(s)** — the single master prompt (plus any sub-prompts) that makes Claude spin up sub-agents, set its own goals/instructions/rubric, and run the **full agentic loop**: build → self-correct → iterate → test → verify "done" against a rubric, with minimal human input.

In short: the deliverable is **idea + the perfect orchestration prompt**, not a product.

## Before I start, three calls that change what the research optimizes for

questions.question: How should the research steer the IDEA we converge on?

options.label: Fully open, highest-EV
options.description: Let research find the single highest-scoring idea against the rubric + prohibited list, no domain constraint. Most exploratory.

options.label: Leverage my existing tools
options.description: Bias toward ideas that build on webpilot / geopilot / TurboWebFetch / Orbit etc., so we start with a head start and a real backlog to point agents at.

options.label: Public-data / SF / nonprofit
options.description: Steer toward the 'tool SF or a nonprofit deserves, built on public data' thought-starter — strong Impact narrative.

options.label: Agent-swarm showcase
options.description: Lean into the 'swarm: point agents at a real backlog' starter — maximizes the Orchestration + Opus 4.8 Use scores explicitly.

questions.question: What's the team/scope assumption I should size the idea and prompt for?

options.label: Solo (you)
options.description: Size the idea so one person + Claude can ship a live demo by 5PM. Tighter scope, leans harder on orchestration doing the work.

options.label: Team of 2-4
options.description: Assume teammates. Idea can be more ambitious; prompt can fan out more parallel work.

questions.question: Where should I save the research documentation for grounding?

options.label: New /research folder here
options.description: Create /Users/azaali/CascadeProjects/hackathon/research/ with one markdown file per agent's findings + a synthesis. Recommended.

options.label: Claude memory dir
options.description: Save into the persistent memory directory so it loads across sessions.

options.label: Both
options.description: Full research docs in /research, plus condensed grounding facts in memory.

aiTitle: Design perfect hackathon prompt with parallel agent research

content.text:
Locked in:
- **Idea steer:** public-data / SF / nonprofit (strongest Impact narrative)
- **Scope:** solo — must ship a live URL by 5PM with Claude doing the heavy lifting
- **Docs:** new `/Users/azaali/CascadeProjects/hackathon/research/`

You answered the questions, so I'm taking that as the green light. Here's the loop I'm launching and then I'll let it run.

**The orchestration:** 10 parallel research agents per round → synthesize a grounding doc + candidate ideas → parallel judges score each against the real rubric and the prohibited list → craft the master prompt → adversarial critics try to break both the idea and the prompt → loop back if anything's fatal. It only converges when a critic confirms we have (1) a locked idea and (2) a self-verifying master prompt for the full agentic loop.

Let me set up the folder and launch.

lastPrompt: use a group of 10 parallel agents in a loop to do all the deep research (and research of previous hackathon github repos for this particular hackathon). save this documentation. to help with grounding…

aiTitle: Design perfect hackathon prompt with parallel agent research

content.text:
The loop is running in the background. Here's exactly what it's doing and how it converges:

**The grounding (saved to `research/`):** Each of the 10 sweep agents writes its own doc (`01-prior-winners.md`, `01-sf-public-data.md`, `01-nonprofit-gaps.md`, `01-dynamic-workflows.md`, etc.), then a synthesis doc (`02-synthesis.md`), and finally the locked answer (`SOLUTION.md`).

**The 10 research lanes** cover the full ground we need: prior Anthropic/CV hackathon winning repos + winning patterns, Claude Build Day / dynamic-workflow exemplars, SF public-data APIs, nonprofit pain points, the dynamic-workflows orchestration mechanics, Opus 4.8 distinctive capabilities, what wins a live 3-min demo, self-verifying "done" patterns, master-prompt engineering, and the fastest solo deploy stack.

**The convergence loop** (this is the part that won't stop early):
1. Sweep → synthesize → generate 5 candidate ideas
2. Parallel harsh judges score each idea on the *real* rubric (Impact 35 / Demo 35 / Opus 15 / Orchestration 15) and flag anything that touches the prohibited list
3. Craft the winning idea's **brief + machine-gradable rubric.md + the single master prompt** for the full agentic loop
4. Three adversarial critics (judge / engineer / orchestration-grader) try to break it
5. Loops back up to 3 rounds, pivoting to the next-best idea if one is judged fatally flawed — **only stops when all critics approve**

The two things it returns are exactly your deliverable: **the idea** and **the master prompt** (plus the rubric and self-verification plan that make Claude run autonomously and catch its own failures).

I'll report back with the converged idea and the prompt when it lands. You can watch live with `/workflows`.

Boundary: no app code.","reasoning-engine (Opus) — pure deterministic evaluate(household, ruleset) walking the branching rule tree, emitting verdict + estimate + per-step citation ids + ca_divergence[]. Boundary: no I/O, no HTTP, deterministic given inputs.","backend (Sonnet) — single Cloudflare Worker: static SPA assets + /healthz ('CalFreshTruth ok') + POST /api/evaluate (calls reasoning-engine) + /referrals (DataSF Mobile Food Schedule jjew-r69b prefetched at build into a static asset). One wrangler deploy. Boundary: serves, doesn't reason.","frontend (Sonnet) — verdict-card SPA wired to /api/evaluate, showing the cited reasoning chain, CA-divergence callouts, and the visible disclaimer. Boundary: presentation only; must actually call the API.","verifier (Opus, fresh-context blackbox) — sees only scenario outputs + acceptance.json oracle + rubric.md; re-grades every verdict, must catch flip-01's federal default, flags only correctness/requirement gaps; the anti-self-preference / anti-laziness / anti-goal-drift guard.","scenario-formatter (Haiku, optional) — mechanical normalization of scenario JSON only; no judgment."],"self_verification_plan":"External, deterministic-first, graded against real end-state — never the agent's self-report. LAYER 1 syntax: node --check on every .mjs (free, deterministic). LAYER 2 oracle suite (test/run.mjs over acceptance.json): ~20 published scenarios with known verdicts; two-sided SWE-bench rule — 100% fail-to-pass (expected verdict + estimate within +/-$15) AND pass-to-pass (no regression); count_passed == count_total or FAIL (agentic-laziness guard); every citation must resolve to a RULESET source id, zero invented ids (agentic-honesty guard). LAYER 3 live end-to-end against the deployed Worker: wrangler deploy, then curl the PRE-COMPUTED workers.dev URL — /healthz==200+'CalFreshTruth ok'; POST /api/evaluate @seed_household.json ==200 with {verdict,estimate,citations,ca_divergence}; fetch / and confirm the SPA renders the card AND actually calls the API (the 'code exists != wired' guard). INDEPENDENT VERIFIER SUB-AGENT (fresh context, blackbox): sees only outputs + oracle + rubric, re-grades each verdict, and on flip-01 must catch the federal-default answer so the walker self-corrects on screen (planted demo moment + self-preferential-bias guard). GATE (model-verifiable, unattended): the /goal condition — node test/run.mjs exits 0 AND curl <URL>/healthz==200 AND verifier reports every rubric task PASS/KNOWN_ISSUE — must hold before emitting <promise>RUBRIC_GREEN</promise>. EVIDENCE not assertion: session_log.md stores captured command output (test summary, curl status codes, flip-01 before/after), not 'it passed.' HARD GUARDRAILS: 3 fix attempts/task -> KNOWN_ISSUE; 12 total iterations; token budget; SUCCESS/FAILURE/BUDGET stop categories; always-run cleanup. RERUNNABLE-TOMORROW PROOF: swap args.ruleset + args.scenarios to a 2-scenario demo-state fixture; the saved .claude/workflows/calfreshtruth.js reruns as /calfreshtruth with new args and passes with zero code edits."}</result>

content.text: All 12 docs landed (~230KB of grounded, cited research). Let me read the SOLUTION so I can give you the actual deliverable.

content.content:
1	# CalFreshTruth — Complete Build-Day Solution
2	
3	> A California-accurate CalFresh eligibility *walker* that reasons step-by-step through CA's exception-laden rules for a household, cites every rule it applied, catches its own federal-default mistakes via an independent verifier, and emits a one-click apply-or-not action sheet. Built behind the Anthropic x Code-for-America SNAP Policy Navigator pattern (announced May 8, 2026).
4	
5	Sources grounding this build:
6	- Anthropic x Code for America SNAP Policy Navigator: https://codeforamerica.org/news/anthropic-partnership/ , https://statescoop.com/code-for-america-anthropic-ai-snap-caseworkers/
7	- CA divergence (the oracle): Modified Categorical Eligibility raises gross income limit to **200% FPL** (federal baseline is **130% FPL**) and **eliminates the asset test** for most households: https://calfresh.guide/categorical-eligibility-in-the-calfresh-program/
8	- DataSF referral layer (Mobile Food Schedule, keyless SODA): https://dev.socrata.com/foundry/data.sfgov.org/jjew-r69b
9	- Deploy contract (single non-interactive `wrangler deploy`, deterministic `workers.dev` URL, static assets + fetch handler in one Worker): https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/ci-cd/external-cicd/github-actions/
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. IDEA BRIEF
14	
15	**Problem.** Generic SNAP screeners give the WRONG answer for California households because CA expanded eligibility far beyond the federal floor: gross-income limit at 200% FPL (vs federal 130%) and no asset test. A moderate-income working household that a federal-rules screener calls "ineligible" is in fact eligible in California. GetCalFresh itself punts ("because eligibility is complex, we recommend you apply"). H.R.1 now financially penalizes states for payment-error rates above 6%, so an *accurate, explainable, CA-correct* walk-through is a live liability for the caseworkers and community orgs who help people apply.
16	
17	**Who it's for.** Low-income California applicants, and the caseworkers / community-org navigators (the Code for America GetCalFresh ecosystem) who help them apply correctly under payment-error liability.
18	
19	**What it does (not a chatbot, not a dashboard, not RAG).** Given a structured household (size, gross monthly income, immigration status of members, shelter/dependent-care/medical expenses, categorical flags), Opus 4.8 walks a conditional rule tree: federal gross-income test -> CA Modified Categorical Eligibility override -> asset test (waived in CA) -> deductions -> net-income test -> special-population rules. It emits a **verdict card**: ELIGIBLE / LIKELY / NOT, an estimated monthly benefit, and a **chain of cited rules** (each step links to the published source, and explicitly flags where CA diverges from federal: "counted under CA rule X, NOT federal rule Y"). An **independent verifier sub-agent** re-grades each verdict against ~20 published example scenarios with known outcomes; if the walker returned the federal-default answer where CA flips it, the verifier fails the check and the orchestrator forces a self-correction before shipping.
20	
21	**What "done" looks like (model-verifiable, no human).**
22	1. `npm test` (or `node test/run.mjs`) exits 0 — all ~20 oracle scenarios produce the correct verdict (fail-to-pass) AND no previously-passing scenario regresses (pass-to-pass).
23	2. A deployed `https://calfreshtruth.<subdomain>.workers.dev` returns **200** on `GET /healthz` (asserts a known string) and **200** on `POST /api/evaluate` with a seed household, returning JSON of shape `{verdict, estimate, citations[], ca_divergence[]}`.
24	3. The planted-fail scenario (`flip-01`) demonstrably shows the verifier catching a federal-default verdict and the walker self-correcting to the CA verdict.
25	4. `rubric.md` scores >= the pass threshold on every criterion when the orchestrator grades itself against the LIVE deployment.
26	5. Artifacts committed: `rubric.md`, `acceptance.json`, `verify.sh`, `RULESET.md` (cited), `PROGRESS.md`, `session_log.md` (with captured command output).
27	
28	**Reframe-tomorrow knob.** Everything is parameterized by `args`: a `ruleset` config (state divergences + sources) and a `scenarios` oracle file. Swap CalFresh for, e.g., New York SNAP or Medi-Cal by replacing two files; the whole orchestration loop reruns unchanged. That is the literal Orchestration-15% "another team reruns it tomorrow on a new problem" criterion.
29	
30	---
31	
32	## 2. rubric.md  (the machine-gradable file the agent grades ITSELF against)
33	
34	```markdown
35	# rubric.md — CalFreshTruth done-gate
36	# DONE = every criterion below is PASS, graded against the LIVE deployment, not the agent's self-report.
37	# Grading is deterministic where a ground truth exists; one llm_rubric slice for groundedness.
38	# Pass threshold: ALL deterministic criteria PASS + groundedness >= 0.85.
39	
40	meta:
41	  pass_threshold: 0.85
42	  max_fix_attempts_per_task: 3
43	  max_total_iterations: 12
44	
45	tasks:
46	
47	  - id: build-syntax
48	    grader: static_analysis
49	    expect: "node --check on every .mjs file exits 0; no unresolved imports"
50	    weight: deterministic
51	
52	  - id: oracle-fail-to-pass
53	    grader: deterministic_tests
54	    test_file: test/run.mjs
55	    expect: "All ~20 scenarios in acceptance.json produce verdict == expected_verdict AND estimate within +/-$15 of expected_estimate"
56	    rule: "PASS only if 100% of scenarios pass (no partial credit — agentic-laziness guard: count_passed == count_total)"
57	    weight: deterministic
58	
59	  - id: oracle-pass-to-pass
60	    grader: deterministic_tests
61	    test_file: test/run.mjs
62	    expect: "No scenario that passed on the previous iteration now fails (regression guard, SWE-bench two-sided rule)"
63	    weight: deterministic
64	
65	  - id: ca-divergence-present
66	    grader: deterministic_tests
67	    expect: "For every scenario whose CA verdict differs from the federal-default verdict, the output ca_divergence[] is non-empty and names the specific CA rule (MCE/200%-FPL or asset-test-waiver)"
68	    weight: deterministic
69	
70	  - id: citations-grounded
71	    grader: deterministic_tests

112	tracked_metrics: [n_iterations, total_subagents, tokens, wall_clock_s]

117	## 3. MASTER PROMPT  (paste into Claude Code; drives the full agentic loop)
118

Do not stop until DONE is proven by captured command output, or you hit the hard caps below and write a TASK_FAILED summary.
121

The differentiator: CA's Modified Categorical Eligibility raises the gross-income limit to 200% FPL (federal baseline 130%) and waives the asset test, so households a federal-rules screener calls "ineligible" are in fact eligible in CA. 126	- Confirm env vars CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID are set (needed for non-interactive `wrangler deploy`). If absent, STOP and ask the user.
127	- Fix the worker name now: "calfreshtruth". Pre-compute the deploy URL as https://calfreshtruth.<account-subdomain>.workers.dev and store it in args. Do NOT parse it from stdout later.
128	- Pass all nondeterministic inputs (timestamps, the worker URL) via args — Date.now(), Math.random(), and argless new Date() THROW inside a workflow.

132	- rubric.md  — exactly the machine-gradable rubric provided to you (tasks with grader types deterministic_tests / state_check / static_analysis / llm_rubric; pass_threshold 0.85; max_fix_attempts_per_task 3; max_total_iterations 12).
133	- RULESET.md — the curated CA-vs-federal rule tree with a SOURCES table (each rule has a stable source id + URL). 134	- acceptance.json — ~20 example household scenarios, each {id, household, expected_verdict, expected_estimate, federal_default_verdict, must_cite[]}. Model routing: Opus for the reasoning engine + verifier + synthesis; Sonnet for backend/frontend/data plumbing; Haiku only for mechanical scenario formatting. Roles:
140	  (a) ruleset-curator [Opus] — fetch + curate RULESET.md and acceptance.json from the cited public sources; return validated JSON; boundary: no app/server code.
141	  (b) reasoning-engine [Opus] — pure function evaluate(household, ruleset) -> {verdict, estimate, citations[], ca_divergence[]}, walking the tree with an explicit branching trace and per-step citation ids; boundary: no I/O, no HTTP, deterministic given inputs.
142	  (c) backend [Sonnet] — one Cloudflare Worker: serves the static SPA AND a fetch handler with /healthz (returns "CalFreshTruth ok"), POST /api/evaluate (calls reasoning-engine), and a /referrals route that reads cached DataSF Mobile Food Schedule (jjew-r69b) prefetched at build into a static asset (keyless SODA GET; no live external call at demo time). 143	  (d) frontend [Sonnet] — the verdict-card SPA: paste/enter a household, show verdict + estimate + the cited reasoning chain with CA-divergence callouts + the visible disclaimer "Screening estimate, not an official eligibility determination." MUST actually call /api/evaluate (wire it; "code exists" != "wired").
144	  (e) verifier [Opus, FRESH CONTEXT, BLACKBOX] — sees ONLY each scenario's output + acceptance.json oracle + rubric.md; re-grades every verdict; for flip-01 it MUST catch a federal-default answer. It did NOT produce the verdicts (no self-preferential bias) and flags ONLY correctness/requirement gaps, not style. Output 0.0-1.0 per criterion + pass/fail + the specific failing assertion.
145	Persist the plan to PROGRESS.md. Sub-agents write artifacts to the filesystem and pass back lightweight refs (minimize telephone).

148	Orchestrator spawns (a)-(d) in parallel where independent (parallel() barrier so synthesis waits for all; .filter(Boolean) dropped failures). Reasoning-engine consumes the curated ruleset. Backend+frontend integrate behind the /api/evaluate contract.

151	Write verify.sh (committed) that the orchestrator RUNS and reports VERBATIM:

Each iteration: read status -> pick ONE failing task -> state the exact failing assertion + one-line root cause -> apply a targeted fix -> re-run verify.sh -> update status + append to results.md. 161	- Re-run the rubric against the LIVE deployment one final time; the /goal condition is: `node test/run.mjs exits 0 AND curl <URL>/healthz==200 AND verifier reports all rubric tasks PASS/KNOWN_ISSUE`.
162	- Prove rerunnable: swap args.ruleset + args.scenarios to a 2-scenario "demo state" fixture and rerun the whole workflow with ZERO code edits; confirm it passes. Save the workflow to .claude/workflows/calfreshtruth.js so it reruns as /calfreshtruth with new args.
163	- Write FINAL.md: the live URL, the rubric scorecard, the flip-01 before/after, and the one-command cold-open (`curl -X POST <URL>/api/evaluate -d @test/seed_household.json`) the 3-min demo opens on.

166	```
167	
168	---
169	
170	## 4. SUB-AGENT ROLES

172	1. **Orchestrator (Opus 4.8, ultracode)** — writes the JS workflow, holds the immutable goal + plan in files, fans out, runs verify.sh verbatim, drives the self-correct loop, gates on `/goal`.
173	2. **ruleset-curator (Opus)** — fetches and curates `RULESET.md` + `acceptance.json` from cited public sources; marks ungroundable rules KNOWN_GAP. Boundary: no app code.
174	3. **reasoning-engine (Opus)** — pure deterministic `evaluate(household, ruleset)` that walks the branching rule tree, emits verdict + estimate + per-step citation ids + `ca_divergence[]`. Boundary: no I/O.
175	4. **backend (Sonnet)** — single Cloudflare Worker: SPA assets + `/healthz` + `POST /api/evaluate` + `/referrals` (prefetched DataSF `jjew-r69b`). Boundary: serves, doesn't reason.
176	5. **frontend (Sonnet)** — verdict-card SPA wired to `/api/evaluate`, citation chain, CA-divergence callouts, visible disclaimer. Boundary: presentation only.
177	6. **verifier (Opus, fresh-context blackbox)** — sees only outputs + oracle + rubric; re-grades every verdict, must catch flip-01's federal default; flags only correctness gaps. The anti-self-preference, anti-laziness, anti-goal-drift guard.
178	7. **(optional) scenario-formatter (Haiku)** — mechanical normalization of scenario JSON only.

180	---

182	## 5. SELF-VERIFICATION PLAN  (how the MODEL proves "done" with no human)
183	
184	**External, deterministic-first, graded against real end-state — never the agent's self-report.**
185	
186	- **Layer 1 — syntax:** `node --check` on every `.mjs` (free, deterministic).
187	- **Layer 2 — oracle test suite (`test/run.mjs` over `acceptance.json`):** ~20 published scenarios with known verdicts. 189	- **Independent verifier sub-agent (fresh context, blackbox):** sees only outputs + oracle + rubric, re-grades each verdict, and on `flip-01` must catch the federal-default answer so the walker self-corrects on screen (the planted demo moment + the self-preferential-bias guard).
190	- **The `/goal` / Stop-hook gate (model-verifiable, unattended):** the run cannot end until `node test/run.mjs` exits 0 AND `curl <URL>/healthz` returns 200 AND the verifier reports every rubric task PASS/KNOWN_ISSUE. Emit `<promise>RUBRIC_GREEN</promise>` only then.

file.content:
# CalFreshTruth — Complete Build-Day Solution

> A California-accurate CalFresh eligibility *walker* that reasons step-by-step through CA's exception-laden rules for a household, cites every rule it applied, catches its own federal-default mistakes via an independent verifier, and emits a one-click apply-or-not action sheet. Built behind the Anthropic x Code-for-America SNAP Policy Navigator pattern (announced May 8, 2026).

Sources grounding this build:
- Anthropic x Code for America SNAP Policy Navigator: https://codeforamerica.org/news/anthropic-partnership/ , https://statescoop.com/code-for-america-anthropic-ai-snap-caseworkers/
- CA divergence (the oracle): Modified Categorical Eligibility raises gross income limit to **200% FPL** (federal baseline is **130% FPL**) and **eliminates the asset test** for most households: https://calfresh.guide/categorical-eligibility-in-the-calfresh-program/
- DataSF referral layer (Mobile Food Schedule, keyless SODA): https://dev.socrata.com/foundry/data.sfgov.org/jjew-r69b
- Deploy contract (single non-interactive `wrangler deploy`, deterministic `workers.dev` URL, static assets + fetch handler in one Worker): https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/ci-cd/external-cicd/github-actions/

---

## 1. IDEA BRIEF

**Problem.** Generic SNAP screeners give the WRONG answer for California households because CA expanded eligibility far beyond the federal floor: gross-income limit at 200% FPL (vs federal 130%) and no asset test. A moderate-income working household that a federal-rules screener calls "ineligible" is in fact eligible in California. GetCalFresh itself punts ("because eligibility is complex, we recommend you apply"). H.R.1 now financially penalizes states for payment-error rates above 6%, so an *accurate, explainable, CA-correct* walk-through is a live liability for the caseworkers and community orgs who help people apply.

**Who it's for.** Low-income California applicants, and the caseworkers / community-org navigators (the Code for America GetCalFresh ecosystem) who help them apply correctly under payment-error liability.

**What it does (not a chatbot, not a dashboard, not RAG).** Given a structured household (size, gross monthly income, immigration status of members, shelter/dependent-care/medical expenses, categorical flags), Opus 4.8 walks a conditional rule tree: federal gross-income test -> CA Modified Categorical Eligibility override -> asset test (waived in CA) -> deductions -> net-income test -> special-population rules. It emits a **verdict card**: ELIGIBLE / LIKELY / NOT, an estimated monthly benefit, and a **chain of cited rules** (each step links to the published source, and explicitly flags where CA diverges from federal: "counted under CA rule X, NOT federal rule Y"). An **independent verifier sub-agent** re-grades each verdict against ~20 published example scenarios with known outcomes; if the walker returned the federal-default answer where CA flips it, the verifier fails the check and the orchestrator forces a self-correction before shipping.

**What "done" looks like (model-verifiable, no human).**
1. `npm test` (or `node test/run.mjs`) exits 0 — all ~20 oracle scenarios produce the correct verdict (fail-to-pass) AND no previously-passing scenario regresses (pass-to-pass).
2. A deployed `https://calfreshtruth.<subdomain>.workers.dev` returns **200** on `GET /healthz` (asserts a known string) and **200** on `POST /api/evaluate` with a seed household, returning JSON of shape `{verdict, estimate, citations[], ca_divergence[]}`.
3. The planted-fail scenario (`flip-01`) demonstrably shows the verifier catching a federal-default verdict and the walker self-correcting to the CA verdict.
4. `rubric.md` scores >= the pass threshold on every criterion when the orchestrator grades itself against the LIVE deployment.
5. Artifacts committed: `rubric.md`, `acceptance.json`, `verify.sh`, `RULESET.md` (cited), `PROGRESS.md`, `session_log.md` (with captured command output).

**Reframe-tomorrow knob.** Everything is parameterized by `args`: a `ruleset` config (state divergences + sources) and a `scenarios` oracle file. Swap CalFresh for, e.g., New York SNAP or Medi-Cal by replacing two files; the whole orchestration loop reruns unchanged. That is the literal Orchestration-15% "another team reruns it tomorrow on a new problem" criterion.

---

## 2. rubric.md  (the machine-gradable file the agent grades ITSELF against)

```markdown
# rubric.md — CalFreshTruth done-gate
# DONE = every criterion below is PASS, graded against the LIVE deployment, not the agent's self-report.
# Grading is deterministic where a ground truth exists; one llm_rubric slice for groundedness.
# Pass threshold: ALL deterministic criteria PASS + groundedness >= 0.85.

meta:
  pass_threshold: 0.85
  max_fix_attempts_per_task: 3
  max_total_iterations: 12

tasks:

  - id: build-syntax
    grader: static_analysis
    expect: "node --check on every .mjs file exits 0; no unresolved imports"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: oracle-fail-to-pass
    grader: deterministic_tests
    test_file: test/run.mjs
    expect: "All ~20 scenarios in acceptance.json produce verdict == expected_verdict AND estimate within +/-$15 of expected_estimate"
    rule: "PASS only if 100% of scenarios pass (no partial credit — agentic-laziness guard: count_passed == count_total)"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: oracle-pass-to-pass
    grader: deterministic_tests
    test_file: test/run.mjs
    expect: "No scenario that passed on the previous iteration now fails (regression guard, SWE-bench two-sided rule)"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: ca-divergence-present
    grader: deterministic_tests
    expect: "For every scenario whose CA verdict differs from the federal-default verdict, the output ca_divergence[] is non-empty and names the specific CA rule (MCE/200%-FPL or asset-test-waiver)"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: citations-grounded
    grader: deterministic_tests
    expect: "Every citation in citations[] resolves to a known source id present in RULESET.md sources table; zero dangling/invented citation ids"
    rule: "Agentic-honesty guard: a verdict with any unresolvable citation is a FAIL, not a warning"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: planted-fail-self-correct
    grader: state_check
    expect: "Running scenario flip-01 through the orchestrator: verifier sub-agent flags the first (federal-default) verdict as WRONG vs oracle, walker re-runs with CA rules, final verdict == oracle. session_log.md contains the captured flip (before: NOT, after: ELIGIBLE)."
    weight: deterministic

  - id: live-health
    grader: state_check
    expect: "curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://<WORKER_URL>/healthz == 200 AND body contains 'CalFreshTruth ok'"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: live-api
    grader: state_check
    expect: "curl -s -X POST https://<WORKER_URL>/api/evaluate -d @test/seed_household.json returns 200 and JSON with keys verdict, estimate, citations, ca_divergence"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: live-end-to-end
    grader: state_check
    expect: "The deployed SPA at https://<WORKER_URL>/ renders the verdict card for the seed household (the 'code exists != code is wired' guard: front end actually calls /api/evaluate and shows citations)"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: screening-not-determination
    grader: deterministic_tests
    expect: "Every API response and the UI include the visible disclaimer string 'Screening estimate, not an official eligibility determination.'"
    weight: deterministic

  - id: groundedness
    grader: llm_rubric
    rubric_prompt: prompts/groundedness.md
    expect: "A fresh-context judge confirms each cited rule's plain-language summary matches the cited source text; score 0.0-1.0 per scenario, mean >= 0.85"
    weight: subjective

  - id: rerunnable
    grader: deterministic_tests
    expect: "Swapping args.ruleset + args.scenarios to a second fixture (a trivial 2-scenario 'demo state') and rerunning the workflow produces a passing run with zero code edits"
    weight: deterministic

tracked_metrics: [n_iterations, total_subagents, tokens, wall_clock_s]
```

---

## 3. MASTER PROMPT  (paste into Claude Code; drives the full agentic loop)

```
  (e) verifier [Opus, FRESH CONTEXT, BLACKBOX] — sees ONLY each scenario's output + acceptance.json oracle + rubric.md; re-grades every verdict; for flip-01 it MUST catch a federal-default answer. It did NOT produce the verdicts (no self-preferential bias) and flags ONLY correctness/requirement gaps, not style. Output 0.0-1.0 per criterion + pass/fail + the specific failing assertion.
Persist the plan to PROGRESS.md. Sub-agents write artifacts to the filesystem and pass back lightweight refs (minimize telephone).

== STEP 3: BUILD ==
Orchestrator spawns (a)-(d) in parallel where independent (parallel() barrier so synthesis waits for all; .filter(Boolean) dropped failures). Reasoning-engine consumes the curated ruleset. Backend+frontend integrate behind the /api/evaluate contract.

== STEP 4: VERIFY (external, 3-layer; NEVER self-assess) ==
Write verify.sh (committed) that the orchestrator RUNS and reports VERBATIM:
  L1 syntax: node --check all .mjs.
  L2 tests: node test/run.mjs over acceptance.json — assert verdict + estimate(+/-$15) for ALL scenarios (fail-to-pass) AND no regression (pass-to-pass). "You MUST run the COMPLETE suite and report ALL failures before marking PASSED." count_passed == count_total or it is a FAIL.
  L3 live end-to-end: `wrangler deploy`, then curl the PRE-COMPUTED URL: /healthz==200+"CalFreshTruth ok"; POST /api/evaluate @test/seed_household.json ==200 with keys {verdict,estimate,citations,ca_divergence}; fetch / and assert the SPA renders the card and calls the API.
Then run the verifier sub-agent (fresh context) to grade against rubric.md including the flip-01 planted-fail and the groundedness slice. Paste all captured output (pytest-style summary, curl status codes, the before/after verdict flip) into session_log.md as EVIDENCE — assertions are not accepted, only output.

== STEP 5: SELF-CORRECT LOOP-UNTIL-DONE (files-as-memory, hard guardrails) ==
Maintain status.json (per-task status / fixAttempts / lastFailingAssertion). Each iteration: read status -> pick ONE failing task -> state the exact failing assertion + one-line root cause -> apply a targeted fix -> re-run verify.sh -> update status + append to results.md. Caps the agent CANNOT edit: max 3 fix attempts per task (then mark KNOWN_ISSUE with reason), max 12 total iterations, a token budget. Stop categories: SUCCESS (all rubric tasks PASS / KNOWN_ISSUE and live URL green) -> emit <promise>RUBRIC_GREEN</promise>; FAILURE -> write "TASK_FAILED: <reason>"; BUDGET -> stop + summarize. ALWAYS run a cleanup step on any exit so the repo is never half-built.

== STEP 6: PROVE DONE + RERUNNABILITY ==
- Re-run the rubric against the LIVE deployment one final time; the /goal condition is: `node test/run.mjs exits 0 AND curl <URL>/healthz==200 AND verifier reports all rubric tasks PASS/KNOWN_ISSUE`.
- Prove rerunnable: swap args.ruleset + args.scenarios to a 2-scenario "demo state" fixture and rerun the whole workflow with ZERO code edits; confirm it passes. Save the workflow to .claude/workflows/calfreshtruth.js so it reruns as /calfreshtruth with new args.
- Write FINAL.md: the live URL, the rubric scorecard, the flip-01 before/after, and the one-command cold-open (`curl -X POST <URL>/api/evaluate -d @test/seed_household.json`) the 3-min demo opens on.

Begin now with STEP 0, then STEP 1. Do not write app code before rubric.md + acceptance.json + RULESET.md exist and the oracle is proven to discriminate.
```

---

## 4. SUB-AGENT ROLES

1. **Orchestrator (Opus 4.8, ultracode)** — writes the JS workflow, holds the immutable goal + plan in files, fans out, runs verify.sh verbatim, drives the self-correct loop, gates on `/goal`.
2. **ruleset-curator (Opus)** — fetches and curates `RULESET.md` + `acceptance.json` from cited public sources; marks ungroundable rules KNOWN_GAP. Boundary: no app code.
3. **reasoning-engine (Opus)** — pure deterministic `evaluate(household, ruleset)` that walks the branching rule tree, emits verdict + estimate + per-step citation ids + `ca_divergence[]`. Boundary: no I/O.
4. **backend (Sonnet)** — single Cloudflare Worker: SPA assets + `/healthz` + `POST /api/evaluate` + `/referrals` (prefetched DataSF `jjew-r69b`). Boundary: serves, doesn't reason.
5. **frontend (Sonnet)** — verdict-card SPA wired to `/api/evaluate`, citation chain, CA-divergence callouts, visible disclaimer. Boundary: presentation only.
6. **verifier (Opus, fresh-context blackbox)** — sees only outputs + oracle + rubric; re-grades every verdict, must catch flip-01's federal default; flags only correctness gaps. The anti-self-preference, anti-laziness, anti-goal-drift guard.
7. **(optional) scenario-formatter (Haiku)** — mechanical normalization of scenario JSON only.

---

## 5. SELF-VERIFICATION PLAN  (how the MODEL proves "done" with no human)

**External, deterministic-first, graded against real end-state — never the agent's self-report.**

- **Layer 1 — syntax:** `node --check` on every `.mjs` (free, deterministic).
- **Layer 2 — oracle test suite (`test/run.mjs` over `acceptance.json`):** ~20 published scenarios with known verdicts. Two-sided SWE-bench rule: 100% fail-to-pass (every scenario hits expected verdict + estimate within +/-$15) AND pass-to-pass (no regression). `count_passed == count_total` or FAIL (agentic-laziness guard). Citations must resolve to RULESET source ids (zero invented ids — agentic-honesty guard).
- **Layer 3 — live end-to-end against the deployed Worker:** `wrangler deploy`, then curl the PRE-COMPUTED `workers.dev` URL: `/healthz` == 200 + "CalFreshTruth ok"; `POST /api/evaluate @seed_household.json` == 200 with `{verdict,estimate,citations,ca_divergence}`; fetch `/` and confirm the SPA renders the card and actually calls the API (the "code exists != wired" guard).
- **Independent verifier sub-agent (fresh context, blackbox):** sees only outputs + oracle + rubric, re-grades each verdict, and on `flip-01` must catch the federal-default answer so the walker self-corrects on screen (the planted demo moment + the self-preferential-bias guard).
- **The `/goal` / Stop-hook gate (model-verifiable, unattended):** the run cannot end until `node test/run.mjs` exits 0 AND `curl <URL>/healthz` returns 200 AND the verifier reports every rubric task PASS/KNOWN_ISSUE. Emit `<promise>RUBRIC_GREEN</promise>` only then.
- **Evidence, not assertion:** `session_log.md` stores captured command output (test summary, curl status codes, the flip-01 before/after verdict), not "it passed."
- **Hard guardrails:** 3 fix attempts/task -> KNOWN_ISSUE; 12 total iterations; token budget; SUCCESS/FAILURE/BUDGET stop categories; always-run cleanup.
- **Rerunnable-tomorrow proof:** swap `args.ruleset` + `args.scenarios` to a 2-scenario demo-state fixture; the saved `.claude/workflows/calfreshtruth.js` reruns as `/calfreshtruth` with new args and passes with zero code edits.

aiTitle: Design perfect hackathon prompt with parallel agent research

content.text:
Done. The loop converged on round 1 with all three adversarial critics (judge, engineer, orchestration-grader) approving. 20 agents, ~990K tokens, ~19 min. Everything's saved in `research/` — 10 cited grounding docs, a synthesis, and `SOLUTION.md` (the deliverable).

## The idea: CalFreshTruth

A **California-accurate CalFresh (SNAP) eligibility *walker***. Not a chatbot, not a dashboard, not RAG — it's multi-step branching reasoning with cited rules and a self-checking verifier.

**The wedge the research found (this is what makes it win):** Generic SNAP screeners give *wrong* answers in California, because CA's Modified Categorical Eligibility raises the gross-income limit to **200% FPL** (federal floor is 130%) and **waives the asset test entirely**. So a working household that a federal-rules screener calls "ineligible" is actually eligible in CA. GetCalFresh itself punts ("eligibility is complex, just apply"). And it's *timely*: it rides directly behind the **Anthropic × Code for America SNAP Policy Navigator** announced May 8, 2026, and H.R.1 now penalizes states for >6% payment-error rates — so accurate, explainable interpretation is a live liability.

**Why it scores on all four criteria:**
- **Impact (35%):** real low-income applicants + the caseworker/navigator ecosystem; built on published public rules + DataSF food-referral data.
- **Demo (35%):** the planted `flip-01` scenario — a household the federal default calls NOT eligible, where the **independent verifier catches the model's own federal-default mistake and the walker self-corrects to ELIGIBLE on screen.** That's the exact "Claude caught and fixed its own failure" moment the judges explicitly asked for.
- **Opus 4.8 (15%):** branching legal-rule reasoning with per-step citations + a fresh-context blackbox verifier — well beyond a basic API call.
- **Orchestration (15%):** "done" is verifiable *by the model* (oracle test suite exits 0, live URL returns 200, gradable `rubric.md`), and it's **rerunnable tomorrow on a new problem** — swap `args.ruleset` + `args.scenarios` to do NY SNAP or Medi-Cal with zero code edits.

It also threads the prohibited list cleanly: explicitly screening-not-determination (not a medical/advice bot), not a dashboard, not basic RAG.

## The deliverable: the master prompt

`SOLUTION.md` contains the complete, paste-ready package:
1. **The idea brief** (problem / who / what "done" looks like)
2. **`rubric.md`** — the machine-gradable done-gate (deterministic tests + state checks + one groundedness slice; pass threshold 0.85; 3 fix-attempts/task, 12-iteration cap)
3. **THE master prompt** — a single `ultracode` prompt with 7 steps: preconditions → write-the-contract-first (eval-driven) → decompose into roled sub-agents (Opus reasoning-engine + verifier, Sonnet backend/frontend, Haiku formatter) → build → 3-layer external verify → self-correct-loop-until-done → prove done + rerunnability. It emits `<promise>RUBRIC_GREEN</promise>` only when tests pass *and* the live URL is green *and* the verifier signs off.

The whole thing is engineered so Claude drives itself: writes its own oracle, proves the oracle discriminates *before* trusting it, builds, deploys to a real Cloudflare Workers URL, curls it to self-verify, and loops on failure — no human in the loop.

Two things worth flagging before build day:
- The master prompt's Step 0 requires `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` + `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` for non-interactive deploy. Have those ready or it'll stop and ask.
- The ~20 oracle scenarios in `acceptance.json` are the spine of the whole thing — the research grounded them in `calfresh.guide` and CDSS sources, but it's worth eyeballing the expected verdicts before the run trusts them.

Want me to do anything with this — e.g. pressure-test the master prompt against the `01-prior-winners.md` patterns one more time, or tighten the demo script?

(6) ROBUSTNESS (G6): pass^3 on the demo contract + one pass on a second contract, against the deterministic CACHED path (not a cold fan-out) so the live demo can't dice-roll red, and the <10s SLA holds. (7) BOUNDED SELF-CORRECTION: <=3 fix attempts per failing grader then known_issue; <=12 iterations or wall-clock budget; state tracked in status.json/results.md/progress.md (also the session-log deliverable). (8) RERUN TOMORROW: packaged as a Skill; swap contract+playbook args -> workflow regenerates artifacts; git clone && ./verify.sh reruns the gate — the .js is a template, the rubric is the contract. RUNTIME SPLIT (resolves the contradiction): the swarm runs OFFLINE as .claude/workflows/redline.js producing verified artifacts; the Cloudflare Worker serves only /health + precomputed /review (<2s) + the redlined-document hero, and never runs the swarm (free plan caps external subrequests at 50 and the request wall-clock binds), so the highest-weight live graders sit on something fast and deterministic."}</result>

content.text:
All 12 docs landed in `research 2/` (~185KB), and `SOLUTION.md` is the deliverable (321 lines, including the full master prompt). Took ~28 min, 24 agents, ~1.1M tokens, and this one needed a second round — the critics caught a real flaw on round 1 and forced a fix.

## Idea two: Redline

**Parallel clause agents redline any contract against your playbook.** A broad/highest-EV, B2B legal-ops play — completely distinct from idea one.

**The problem:** contract review is 70-85% of lawyer-hours per agreement, manual, and inconsistent against a company's own negotiation playbook. **The mechanic:** drop in a real commercial contract + a structured playbook (preferred / acceptable-fallback / non-negotiable per clause type). An orchestrator fans out **one Opus agent per clause category** (Indemnification, Limitation of Liability, IP Assignment, Termination, Data Protection, Governing Law). Each finds and judges its clauses against its playbook section and emits issues that quote the contract **verbatim**. An **adversarial verifier re-greps every citation against the source** and bounces any drifted quote for re-extraction. Output is a **redlined HTML document** + risk-ranked issues + deal-breakers — not a dashboard, not basic RAG (whole contract in 1M context, clause-level judgment, produces an *edited artifact*).

**Why it scores:**
- **Impact (35%):** dominant cost center in legal; credible legal-tech design-partner path.
- **Demo (35%):** the hero is a real redlined document, and the wow moment (**G5**) is the verifier catching a drifted/fabricated citation and forcing a verbatim re-extract on screen — the "Claude caught and fixed its own failure" beat.
- **Opus 4.8 (15%):** 1M-context whole-contract reasoning + a 6-way parallel clause swarm + adversarial self-verification.
- **Orchestration (15%):** "done" = **the exit code of `verify.sh`**, never the model's opinion.

**The thing that makes this round-2 version genuinely strong** (the flaws the critics caught and the rebuild fixed):
1. **Self-grading lock** — `rubric.md` is frozen in Phase 0 and SHA-256-checksummed; `verify.sh` exits non-zero if the checksum drifts, so **the agent literally cannot move its own pass-bar.**
2. **Honest oracle** — they discovered CUAD's real metric is Precision@80%Recall (~0.44 baseline), so the naive "recall ≥ 0.70" gate was *unreachable* and would loop forever; replaced with a calibrated, reachability-smoke-tested bar. And the scope is honestly stated: CUAD certifies clause *detection*, not redline quality (legal-ops assist, not autonomous legal advice).
3. **Runtime split** — the swarm runs **offline** as `.claude/workflows/redline.js` producing verified artifacts; the Cloudflare Worker serves only `/health` + precomputed `/review` + the redlined-document hero (Workers free plan caps external subrequests at 50, so the live demo can't dice-roll red).
- **Rights are clean:** CUAD is CC BY 4.0, SEC EDGAR Exhibit-10 contracts are public domain.
- **Rerunnable tomorrow:** packaged as a Skill — swap the contract + playbook args, `git clone && ./verify.sh` reruns the gate on a new contract.

The full idea brief, the checksum-locked rubric, the master prompt, and the verification plan are in `research 2/SOLUTION.md`.

## You now have two complete, distinct solutions

| | **Idea 1 — CalFreshTruth** (`research/`) | **Idea 2 — Redline** (`research 2/`) |
|---|---|---|
| Angle | SF / nonprofit / public-data | Broad / highest-EV B2B legal-ops |
| Core | CA-accurate SNAP eligibility walker | Per-clause contract redlining swarm |
| Wow moment | Verifier catches federal-default mistake, walker self-corrects | Verifier catches drifted citation, forces verbatim re-extract |
| Impact story | Real applicants + caseworkers; rides Anthropic × Code for America | $-heavy legal cost center; design-partner path |
| Self-grade integrity | rubric + oracle-discriminates check | **rubric SHA-256-locked** so the agent can't move its bar |

Want me to do a head-to-head — have a panel score both against the rubric and pick the one most likely to win, or generate a third along a different axis (e.g. dev-tools / creative)?
